Security.0103.description: Certificate management packages or configurations detected. This may indicate the application is using local or custom certificate handling, which could require adjustments when migrating to Azure. Consider using Azure Key Vault Certificates for secure storage, lifecycle management, and integration with Azure services.
Security.0103.title: Certificate management dependency detected

Local.0003.description:
Your application has local or network IO operations.

If you are targeting Managed Instance on Azure App Service, move persistent I/O to storage mounts (Azure Files/UNC). Keep local writes to temp only; connect to external dependencies via private endpoints/VNet to reduce latency.

IO operations outside of the application folder might not be available on Azure App Service. Review and ensure IO APIs are attempting to access paths that will be available when your app is running on App Service sandbox. If you choose the containerized hosting option, your scenario still might be broken or have scalability issues.

Possible solutions:
    1. Files that the application needs can be deployed with the application or put in Azure Storage. You can use Azure Storage File Shares as a drop-in replacement for on-premises file share.
    2. Other services that the application depends on might need to be migrated to Azure, this way you will get the benefit of lower latency in the cloud.
    3. If your application needs to depend on services that run on-premise, you can connect your Azure and local networks with ExpressRoute, Hybrid Connections, or VPN solutions.
Local.0003.title: Local or network IO operations detected

Local.0021.description:
Dynamic assembly loading has been detected.

Make sure the loaded assembly is accessible in the cloud environment.

Note that incidents are not detected in dynamically loaded assemblies.
Local.0021.title: Detected loading of dynamic assemblies.

Local.0006.description:
Your application has hardcoded URLs that might correspond to services that are not accessible on Azure.

Review and ensure that the application is attempting to access URLs that would be available from Azure. You might need to migrate those services to Azure too, this way you will get the benefit of lower latency in the cloud.

If your application needs to depend on services that run on-premises, you can connect your Azure and local networks with ExpressRoute, Hybrid Connections, or VPN solutions.
Local.0006.title: Hardcoded URLs detected

HTTP.0001.description:
Your application is trying to reach external resources via HTTP protocol.

For Managed Instance on App Service, secure connectivity to internal/on-prem HTTP services is managed via plan-level VNet integration and private endpoints. Keep inbound traffic restricted to 80/443; capture any required HTTP config in install scripts. Use Bastion JIT RDP only for diagnostics—persist all changes via install scripts. Outbound traffic does not impose similar restrictions.

While if targeting other Azure compute services, ensure the resources it is trying to reach are accessible from the Azure environment. If those HTTP requests are sent to internal services or services hosted on-prem, you probably will need to migrate those services to Azure as well.

Also, keep in mind that Azure App Service supports port 80 for HTTP and port 443 for HTTPS traffic. If you are using any other ports, you need to update them before moving to Azure.
HTTP.0001.title: Access to external resources via HTTP is detected

Identity.0001.description:
The authentication you are using is not supported in Azure.

Instead, you can use the built-in authentication feature of Azure App Service, which provides out-of-the-box authentication with various identity providers. Also, any OpenID Connect provider can be used for authentication by specifying the sign-in endpoint.

If there is no existing authentication provider that suits your scenario, you could use your own custom authentication solution or the bundled security features in your web framework of choice.
Identity.0001.title: Unsupported authentication type detected

links.title: Moving apps with Windows auth to Azure App Service

Cache.0001.description:
Your application is using a data cache.

Some caching solutions will only work on the local machine or within an on-premises network. Make sure that the cache your app is working with is available from Azure.

Use the link below to learn how caching solutions could be implemented in Azure.
Cache.0001.title: Data caching is detected

Queue.0001.description:
You are using RabbitMQ service which does not exist in Azure by default. To use RabbitMQ with Azure App Service and containerized app, you have a few options. Each option has its own advantages and disadvantages, depending on your requirements, budget, and preferences:

    1. You can use Azure Service Bus, a fully managed messaging service that offers queues, topics, and subscriptions. You can integrate Azure Service Bus with RabbitMQ using the Shovel plugin that comes with RabbitMQ.
    2. You can create your own RabbitMQ cluster on Azure using virtual machines or containers. You can either install RabbitMQ manually on each node or use a pre-configured solution like Bitnami RabbitMQ Cluster. You can then connect to your RabbitMQ cluster from your Azure App Service app using the load balancer IP or hostname.
    3. You can use a third-party service like CloudAMQP or other, that provides RabbitMQ as a service on Azure. You can create a free or paid plan and connect to your RabbitMQ instance from your Azure App Service app using the connection string provided by CloudAMQP.
Queue.0001.title: RabbitMQ usage is detected

Local.0004.description:
Logging to local or network paths detected.

If you are targeting Managed Instance on Azure App Service, prefer Application Insights/App Service logging. If filesystem logs are required, write to a mounted Azure Files share configured via storage mounts and Key Vault secrets.

Logging APIs that write to disk might not work on Azure App Service or Container.

Use logging that stores logs in locations outside of the host unless its specifically required by your app. You can also use logging capabilities embedded in Azure, such as Azure App Service logging or Application Insights.

For migrating to AKS, use LogMonitor tool that can capture logs written to files, the console, or write logs and redirect them to Azure.
Local.0004.title: Logging to local or network paths detected

Logging.0102.description: Serilog logging framework detected. While Serilog is a popular logging framework, consider migrating to OpenTelemetry for better cloud-native observability and standardized telemetry. OpenTelemetry provides unified logging, metrics, and tracing with better Azure integration.
Logging.0102.title: Serilog logging framework detected

Scale.0001.description:
Static content detected.

Your current approach of serving static content directly might lead to increased costs, performance issues, maintenance challenges (requiring application redeployment for content changes), and security risks.

Consider moving static content to Azure Blob Storage and adding Azure CDN.
Scale.0001.title: Static content detected

Local.0013.description:
Shared job storage provider API detected.

If you are targeting Managed Instance on Azure App Service, use Azure Functions (timers/queues), Logic Apps, or Azure Batch; avoid cross-process schedulers tied to local/remote hosts.

While if targeting other Azure compute services, schedulers which are entirely contained in-process are not a concern when moving to Azure, however schedulers working in a distributed way in separate local or remote processes may not migrate well to Azure.

The API below might be used to setup a shared job storage and needs to be reviewed. Azure provides several options to run scheduled or batch jobs including Azure Batch service, Azure Logic Apps or different Azure Functions triggers depending on the job needs.
Local.0013.title: Shared job storage provider detected

Perf.0001.description:
Synchronous API usage detected.

Using synchronous APIs can lead to more performance issues in Azure compared to on-premises environments due to different latency profiles. Consider adopting asynchronous code patterns.

In heavily loaded systems, especially where the I/O is substantial, using asynchronous patterns can improve overall throughput and performance. Asynchronous APIs enable a small pool of threads to manage thousands of concurrent requests without waiting on blocking calls. Instead of waiting for a long-running synchronous task to finish, the thread can handle another request. Conversely, numerous synchronous blocking calls can cause thread pool starvation and degrade response times.

Refer to the links below to learn more about asynchronous programming.
Perf.0001.title: Synchronous API usage detected

links.title: ASP.NET Core Best Practices: avoid blocking calls
